common_name: Galaxy Magnolia (Deep Pink Late-Blooming Hybrid Magnolia)

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 5.5–7.0), fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Galaxy blooms 2–3 weeks later than saucer magnolia, significantly reducing the risk of late frost damage to the large pink flowers.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Galaxy is a USDA National Arboretum hybrid magnolia introduction — a cross between Magnolia liliiflora 'Nigra' (the dark-purple Japanese magnolia) and Magnolia sprengeri 'Diva' (the vivid pink Chinese magnolia) that combines the intense dark-pink to reddish-purple color and late bloom timing of the former with the large flower size and vigorous tree habit of the latter. The large, upright, tulip-shaped flowers (6–8 inches across when fully open) are deep rose-pink to reddish-pink on the exterior and lighter pink on the interior, produced in exceptional profusion on an upright, oval-to-pyramidal tree reaching 20–30 feet at maturity. The bloom timing — 2–3 weeks later than saucer magnolia (Magnolia × soulangeana) — is Galaxy's most practically important quality: in zone 6–7, late frosts frequently damage the flowers of earlier-blooming saucer magnolias; Galaxy's delayed bloom schedule moves past the most dangerous late-frost window. The upright habit and considerable height distinguish Galaxy from low-branching, spreading saucer magnolias — Galaxy has a more tree-like form appropriate for use as a street tree or in more formal landscape settings. In zone 9, Galaxy blooms in February–March with no frost risk, producing an exceptional flower display in warm winter conditions.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Variegated Society Garlic is an outstanding evergreen edible-ornamental for zone 8–10 gardens — the cream and green striped foliage and long-blooming pink flowers provide year-round ornamental interest while the edible leaves offer mild garlic-chive flavor.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Variegated Society Garlic is the ornamentally superior form of Tulbaghia violacea — a South African bulbous perennial producing narrow, strap-like leaves with a striking cream to cream-white longitudinal stripe along the center or edge of each leaf. The two-tone cream-and-green variegated foliage has significantly higher ornamental value than the solid-green species and creates a bright, light-catching effect particularly valuable in partial shade. The long bloom season (May through October in zone 9, often year-round in frost-free winters) produces clusters of small, tubular, lilac-pink to rose-violet flowers on slender scapes 12–18 inches tall — the pink flowers above the cream-variegated foliage create an outstanding three-season color combination. The leaves, stems, and flowers are all edible with a mild garlic-chive flavor — milder than either garlic or chives individually — appropriate for salads, garnishes, and any dish where a delicate garlic note is desired. The common name 'society garlic' reflects this mild, socially acceptable garlic flavor (lacking the persistent breath odor of true garlic). Variegated Society Garlic is drought-tolerant, deer-resistant (due to the garlic scent), and virtually pest-free. In zone 9, it is evergreen year-round in mild winters and recovers quickly from any frost damage. An outstanding dual-purpose plant for the Gulf South garden.

common_name: Louisiana Long Green Eggplant (Old South Elongated Green Eggplant)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Louisiana Long Green is particularly productive in the Gulf South's long, hot growing season — transplant in spring and again in August for maximum productivity.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Louisiana Long Green is a traditional old-South eggplant variety producing elongated, cylindrical fruits 8–12 inches long in an unusual pale green to light green-white color that stands in stark contrast to the standard purple-black American eggplant. The pale green color indicates ripe fruit — unlike purple-black types where color darkens as they ripen, Louisiana Long Green turns a light, creamy green to yellow-green when mature. The flesh is white, mild, and very low in bitterness — outstanding for frying, grilling, and the classic Louisiana preparation of eggplant dressing (a traditional Gulf South casserole combining eggplant with shrimp, crabmeat, bread crumbs, and Cajun seasonings). Louisiana Long Green is a regional heirloom that has been grown continuously in Louisiana and lower Mississippi kitchen gardens for generations — selected by Southern gardeners for its outstanding heat tolerance, prolific production in the Gulf South summer, and excellent culinary qualities in traditional regional recipes. Harvest when fruits are 8–10 inches long with pale, slightly shiny skin — over-mature fruits become yellowish and develop more seeds and bitterness. Plants are vigorous and can reach 4 feet tall in the long Gulf South growing season, requiring staking when heavily loaded with fruit.

common_name: Jupiter Bell Pepper (Large Blocky Red-Ripening Sweet Pepper)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; in zone 9, plant in late February through March and again in August–September for a productive fall crop — peppers often produce best in the cooler temperatures of fall.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun at or just before the last frost date; Jupiter is one of the largest and most productive open-pollinated bell pepper varieties — producing extra-large, four-lobed, thick-walled fruits that are larger and more blocky than standard California Wonder types. The fruits are harvested green for fresh use or allowed to ripen fully to a rich, sweet red (occasionally yellow to orange). Jupiter is prized for its combination of very large fruit size (often 4–5 inches long, 4 inches wide), thick walls that hold up well in cooking, and outstanding sweet flavor that improves markedly when allowed to ripen fully red. As an open-pollinated, non-hybrid variety, Jupiter seeds can be saved from the best fruits for replanting — an important advantage for seed-saving gardeners who want consistent results without purchasing new seed annually. Jupiter's disease tolerance (particularly to tobacco mosaic virus) represents an improvement over older open-pollinated varieties. In zone 9, bell peppers thrive in spring (late February through June) and even more productively in fall (August transplants through November) — the fall crop in zone 9 consistently produces the largest, most flavorful fruits as temperatures moderate from the summer peak. Zone 9 gardeners have two full productive seasons for bell peppers, providing fresh peppers for much of the year.

common_name: Yolo Wonder Bell Pepper (Virus-Resistant Open-Pollinated Sweet Pepper)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Yolo Wonder's virus resistance is a practical advantage in zone 9 where tobacco mosaic virus and pepper mosaic virus persist in soil and infect peppers through insect vectors.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun at or just before the last frost date; Yolo Wonder is one of the most important open-pollinated bell pepper varieties in American horticulture — developed at the University of California, Davis, from California Wonder breeding stock specifically to incorporate resistance to tobacco mosaic virus (TMV), a common disease that devastates susceptible bell pepper varieties throughout warm-season gardens. Yolo Wonder produces large, four-lobed, blocky, thick-walled fruits in the classic California Wonder mold — essentially identical in fruit character to California Wonder but with the added practical advantage of TMV resistance that makes it significantly more reliable in gardens where the virus is endemic in the soil. The fruits ripen from green to red and have full, sweet bell pepper flavor at both stages. Yolo Wonder is the virus-resistant standard for open-pollinated bell pepper production that replaced California Wonder in many commercial plantings when TMV was recognized as a major production challenge. As an open-pollinated variety, seeds can be saved reliably from season to season. The practical combination of virus resistance, large fruit size, thick walls, and excellent flavor has kept Yolo Wonder among the most commonly recommended open-pollinated bell peppers for home gardeners in disease-prone environments throughout the Gulf South.
sources: University of California Agriculture and Natural Resources
